This skill is a router: it tells you which tool to use for each file type (pdf, docx, xlsx, csv, json, images, archives, ebooks) so you read the right amount the right way instead of blindly running cat on a binary. Triggers: any mention of /mnt/user-data/uploads/, an uploaded_files section, a file_path tag, or a user asking about an uploaded file you have not yet read. Do NOT use this skill if the file content is already visible in your context inside a documents block \u2014 you already have it.\"\n     4\tcompatibility: \"claude.ai, Claude Desktop, Cowork \u2014 any surface where uploads land at /mnt/user-data/uploads/\"\n     5\tlicense: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms\n     6\t---\n     7\t\n     8\t# Reading Uploaded Files\n     9\t\n    10\t## Why this skill exists\n    11\t\n    12\tWhen a user uploads a file in claude.ai, Claude Desktop, or Cowork,\n    13\tthe file is written to `/mnt/user-data/uploads/<filename>` and you are told the path\n    14\tin an `<uploaded_files>` block. **The content is not in your context.**\n    15\tYou must go read it.\n    16\t\n    17\tThe naive thing \u2014 `cat /mnt/user-data/uploads/whatever` \u2014 is wrong for\n    18\tmost files:\n    19\t\n    20\t- On a PDF it prints binary garbage.\n    21\t- On a 100MB CSV it floods your context with rows you will never use.\n    22\t- On a DOCX it prints the raw ZIP bytes.\n    23\t- On an image it does nothing useful at all.\n    24\t\n    25\tThis skill tells you the right first move for each type, and when to\n    26\thand off to a deeper skill.\n    27\t\n    28\t## General protocol\n    29\t\n    30\t1. **Look at the extension.** That is your dispatch key.\n    31\t2. **Stat before you read.** Large files need sampling, not slurping.\n    32\t   ```bash\n    33\t   stat -c '%s bytes, %y' /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf\n    34\t   file /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf\n    35\t   ```\n    36\t3. **Read just enough to answer the user's question.** If they asked\n    37\t   \"how many rows are in this CSV\", don't load the whole thing into\n    38\t   pandas \u2014 `wc -l` gives a fast approximation (it counts newlines,\n    39\t   not CSV records, so it may over-count if quoted fields contain\n    40\t   embedded newlines).\n    41\t4. **If a dedicated skill exists, go read it.** The table below tells\n    42\t   you when. The dedicated skills cover editing, creating, and advanced\n    43\t   operations that this skill does not.\n    44\t\n    45\t## `extract-text`\n    46\t\n    47\tFor docx, odt, epub, xlsx, pptx, rtf, and ipynb the first move is\n    48\t`extract-text <file>`. It emits markdown for docx/odt/epub (headings,\n    49\tbold, lists, links, tables), tab-separated rows under `## Sheet:`\n    50\theaders for xlsx, text under `## Slide N` headers for pptx, fenced\n    51\tcode cells for ipynb, and plain text for rtf. Pass `--format <fmt>`\n    52\twhen the extension is wrong or absent (e.g., `--format xlsx` on an\n    53\t`.xlsm`). If it errors on a file, `pandoc <file> -t plain` is a\n    54\tfallback; for xlsx/pptx, fall back to the dedicated skill's\n    55\tPython-based approach (openpyxl / python-pptx).\n    56\t\n    57\t## Dispatch table\n    58\t\n    59\t| Extension                         | First move                                           | Dedicated skill                           |\n    60\t| --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |\n    61\t| `.pdf`                            | Content inventory (see PDF section)                  | `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md` |\n    62\t| `.docx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |\n    63\t| `.doc` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.docx` first                             | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |\n    64\t| `.xlsx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |\n    65\t| `.xlsm`                           | `extract-text --format xlsx`                         | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |\n    66\t| `.xls` (legacy)                   | `pd.read_excel(engine=\"xlrd\")` \u2014 openpyxl rejects it | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |\n    67\t| `.ods`                            | `pd.read_excel(engine=\"odf\")` \u2014 openpyxl rejects it  | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |\n    68\t| `.pptx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |\n    69\t| `.ppt` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.pptx` first                             | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |\n    70\t| `.csv`, `.tsv`                    | `pandas` with `nrows`                                | \u2014 (below)                                 |\n    71\t| `.json`, `.jsonl`                 | `jq` for structure                                   | \u2014 (below)                                 |\n    72\t| `.jpg`, `.png`, `.gif`, `.webp`   | Already in your context as vision input              | \u2014 (below)                                 |\n    73\t| `.zip`, `.tar`, `.tar.gz`         | List contents, do **not** auto-extract               | \u2014 (below)                                 |\n    74\t| `.gz` (single file)               | `zcat \\| head` \u2014 no manifest to list                 | \u2014 (below)                                 |\n    75\t| `.epub`, `.odt`                   | `extract-text`                                       | \u2014 (below)                                 |\n    76\t| `.rtf`                            | `extract-text`                                       | \u2014 (below)                                 |\n    77\t| `.ipynb`                          | `extract-text`                                       | \u2014 (below)                                 |\n    78\t| `.txt`, `.md`, `.log`, code files | `wc -c` then `head` or full `cat`                    | \u2014 (below)                                 |\n    79\t| Unknown                           | `file` then decide                                   | \u2014                                         |\n    80\t\n    81\t---\n    82\t\n    83\t## PDF\n    84\t\n    85\t**Never** `cat` a PDF \u2014 it prints binary garbage.\n    86\t\n    87\tQuick first move \u2014 get the page count and check if text is extractable:\n    88\t\n    89\t```bash\n    90\tpdfinfo /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf\n    91\tpdftotext -f 1 -l 1 /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf - | head -20\n    92\t```\n    93\t\n    94\tThen peek at the text content:\n    95\t\n    96\t```python\n    97\tfrom pypdf import PdfReader\n    98\tr = PdfReader(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf\")\n    99\tprint(f\"{len(r.pages)} pages\")\n   100\tprint(r.pages[0].extract_text()[:2000])\n   101\t```\n   102\t\n   103\tFor anything beyond a quick peek \u2014 figures, tables, attachments,\n   104\tforms, scanned PDFs, visual inspection, or choosing a reading strategy\n   105\t\u2014 go read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md`. It covers\n   106\tcontent inventory, text extraction vs. page rasterization, embedded\n   107\tcontent extraction, and document-type-aware reading strategies.\n   108\t\n   109\tFor PDF form filling, creation, merging, splitting, or watermarking,\n   110\tgo read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf/SKILL.md`.\n   111\t\n   112\t---\n   113\t\n   114\t## DOCX / DOC\n   115\t\n   116\tThe `docx` skill covers editing, creating, tracked changes, images.\n   117\tRead it if you need any of those. For a quick look:\n   118\t\n   119\t```bash\n   120\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/memo.docx | head -200\n   121\t```\n   122\t\n   123\tLegacy `.doc` (not `.docx`) must be converted first \u2014 see the `docx`\n   124\tskill.\n   125\t\n   126\t---\n   127\t\n   128\t## XLSX / XLS / spreadsheets\n   129\t\n   130\tThe `xlsx` skill covers formulas, formatting, charts, creating. Read\n   131\tit if you need any of those. For a quick look at an `.xlsx`:\n   132\t\n   133\t```bash\n   134\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.xlsx | head -100\n   135\t```\n   136\t\n   137\tFor `.xlsm`, add `--format xlsx` (same zip structure; only the\n   138\textension differs). When you need a structured preview in Python:\n   139\t\n   140\t```python\n   141\tfrom openpyxl import load_workbook\n   142\twb = load_workbook(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.xlsx\", read_only=True)\n   143\tprint(\"Sheets:\", wb.sheetnames)\n   144\tws = wb.active\n   145\tfor row in ws.iter_rows(max_row=5, values_only=True):\n   146\t    print(row)\n   147\t```\n   148\t\n   149\t`read_only=True` matters \u2014 without it, openpyxl loads the entire\n   150\tworkbook into memory, which breaks on large files. Do not trust\n   151\t`ws.max_row` in read-only mode: many non-Excel writers omit the\n   152\tdimension record, so it comes back `None` or wrong. If you need a row\n   153\tcount, iterate or use pandas.\n   154\t\n   155\t**Legacy `.xls`** \u2014 openpyxl raises `InvalidFileException`. Use:\n   156\t\n   157\t```python\n   158\timport pandas as pd\n   159\tdf = pd.read_excel(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/old.xls\", engine=\"xlrd\", nrows=5)\n   160\t```\n   161\t\n   162\t**`.ods` (OpenDocument)** \u2014 openpyxl also rejects this. Use:\n   163\t\n   164\t```python\n   165\timport pandas as pd\n   166\tdf = pd.read_excel(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.ods\", engine=\"odf\", nrows=5)\n   167\t```\n   168\t\n   169\t---\n   170\t\n   171\t## PPTX\n   172\t\n   173\t```bash\n   174\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/deck.pptx | head -200\n   175\t```\n   176\t\n   177\t**Legacy `.ppt`** \u2014 convert to `.pptx` first via LibreOffice; see\n   178\t`/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md` for the sandbox-safe\n   179\t`scripts/office/soffice.py` wrapper (bare `soffice` hangs here because\n   180\tthe seccomp filter blocks the `AF_UNIX` sockets LibreOffice uses for\n   181\tinstance management).\n   182\t\n   183\tFor anything beyond reading, go to `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`.\n   184\t\n   185\t---\n   186\t\n   187\t## CSV / TSV\n   188\t\n   189\t**Do not** `cat` or `head` these blindly. A CSV with a 50KB quoted cell\n   190\tin row 1 will wreck your `head -5`. Use pandas with `nrows`:\n   191\t\n   192\t```python\n   193\timport pandas as pd\n   194\tdf = pd.read_csv(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv\", nrows=5)\n   195\tprint(df)\n   196\tprint()\n   197\tprint(df.dtypes)\n   198\t```\n   199\t\n   200\tApproximate row count without loading (over-counts if the file has\n   201\tRFC-4180 quoted newlines \u2014 the same quoted-cell case this section\n   202\twarned about above):\n   203\t\n   204\t```bash\n   205\twc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv\n   206\t```\n   207\t\n   208\tFull analysis only after you know the shape:\n   209\t\n   210\t```python\n   211\tdf = pd.read_csv(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv\")\n   212\tprint(df.describe())\n   213\t```\n   214\t\n   215\tTSV: same, with `sep=\"\\t\"`.\n   216\t\n   217\t---\n   218\t\n   219\t## JSON / JSONL\n   220\t\n   221\tStructure first, content second:\n   222\t\n   223\t```bash\n   224\tjq 'type' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json\n   225\tjq 'if type == \"array\" then length elif type == \"object\" then keys else . end' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json\n   226\t```\n   227\t\n   228\t(`keys` errors on scalar JSON roots \u2014 a bare `\"hello\"` or `42` is valid\n   229\tJSON per RFC 7159 \u2014 so guard the branch.)\n   230\t\n   231\tThen drill into what the user actually asked about.\n   232\t\n   233\tJSONL (one object per line) \u2014 do **not** `jq` the whole file; work line\n   234\tby line:\n   235\t\n   236\t```bash\n   237\thead -3 /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl | jq .\n   238\twc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl\n   239\t```\n   240\t\n   241\t---\n   242\t\n   243\t## Images (JPG / PNG / GIF / WEBP)\n   244\t\n   245\t**You can already see uploaded images.** They are injected into your\n   246\tcontext as vision inputs alongside the `<uploaded_files>` pointer. You\n   247\tdo not need to read them from disk to describe them.\n   248\t\n   249\tThe disk copy is only needed if you are going to **process** the image\n   250\tprogrammatically:\n   251\t\n   252\t```python\n   253\tfrom PIL import Image\n   254\timg = Image.open(\"/mnt/user-data/uploads/photo.jpg\")\n   255\tprint(img.size, img.mode, img.format)\n   256\t```\n   257\t\n   258\tFor OCR on an image (text extraction, not description):\n   259\t\n   260\t```python\n   261\timport pytesseract\n   262\tprint(pytesseract.image_to_string(img))\n   263\t```\n   264\t\n   265\tNote: the client resizes images larger than 2000\u00d72000 down to that\n   266\tbound and re-encodes as JPEG before upload, so the disk copy may not\n   267\tbe the user's original bytes. For most processing this doesn't matter;\n   268\tif the user is asking about original-resolution pixel data, flag it.\n   269\t\n   270\t---\n   271\t\n   272\t## Archives (ZIP / TAR / TAR.GZ)\n   273\t\n   274\t**List first. Extract never \u2014 unless the user explicitly asks.**\n   275\tArchives can be huge, contain path traversal, or nest forever.\n   276\t\n   277\t```bash\n   278\tunzip -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip\n   279\ttar -tf /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.tar\n   280\t```\n   281\t\n   282\tGNU tar auto-detects compression \u2014 `tar -tf` works on `.tar`,\n   283\t`.tar.gz`, `.tar.bz2`, `.tar.xz` alike. Don't hard-code `-z`.\n   284\t\n   285\tIf the user wants one file from inside, extract just that one:\n   286\t\n   287\t```bash\n   288\tunzip -p /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip path/inside/file.txt\n   289\t```\n   290\t\n   291\t**Standalone `.gz`** (not a tar) compresses a single file \u2014 there is\n   292\tno manifest to list. Just peek at the decompressed content:\n   293\t\n   294\t```bash\n   295\tzcat /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json.gz | head -50\n   296\t```\n   297\t\n   298\t---\n   299\t\n   300\t## EPUB / ODT\n   301\t\n   302\t```bash\n   303\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/book.epub | head -200\n   304\t```\n   305\t\n   306\tFor long ebooks, pipe through `head` \u2014 you rarely need the whole thing\n   307\tto answer a question.\n   308\t\n   309\t---\n   310\t\n   311\t## RTF / IPYNB\n   312\t\n   313\t```bash\n   314\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/notes.rtf | head -200\n   315\textract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/notebook.ipynb | head -200\n   316\t```\n   317\t\n   318\t---\n   319\t\n   320\t## Plain text / code / logs\n   321\t\n   322\tCheck the size first:\n   323\t\n   324\t```bash\n   325\twc -c /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log\n   326\t```\n   327\t\n   328\t- **Under ~20KB**: `cat` is fine.\n   329\t- **Over ~20KB**: `head -100` and `tail -100` to orient. If the user\n   330\t  asked about something specific, `grep` for it. Load the whole thing\n   331\t  only if you genuinely need all of it.\n   332\t\n   333\tFor log files, the user almost always cares about the end:\n   334\t\n   335\t```bash\n   336\ttail -200 /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log\n   337\t```\n   338\t\n   339\t---\n   340\t\n   341\t## Unknown extension\n   342\t\n   343\t```bash\n   344\tfile /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin\n   345\txxd /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin | head -5\n   346\t```\n   347\t\n   348\t`file` identifies most things. `xxd` head shows magic bytes. If `file`\n   349\tsays \"data\" and the hex doesn't match anything you recognize, ask the\n   350\tuser what it is instead of guessing.\n   351\t",

    "input": {
      "description": "Add v0.56 changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.56 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-56\">v0.56 &mdash; Workflow polish &amp; deluge headroom</h2>\n        <p>\n          Five small fixes from real-use feedback. None of them are\n          architectural &mdash; this is the kind of release that\n          accumulates when you actually start using the thing.\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Docked panel no longer auto-hides.</strong> Six event handlers used to call <code>hideUI()</code> when their thing fired: animation change, visualization change, background change, SVG load (non-instant), SVG replay (non-instant), and paintImage. Made sense in the bottom-centered overlay era (v0.52 and earlier) when the panel literally covered the canvas. With the right-docked panel (v0.54+), the canvas area is already stable &mdash; the panel doesn't intrude &mdash; and auto-hiding just made the user press <kbd>H</kbd> to bring it back so they could keep iterating. Removed all six calls. The H-key shortcut and the visible toggle button still hide manually when wanted.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Container W/H inputs match the panel's visual language.</strong> The <code>&lt;input type=\"number\"&gt;</code> elements for container size were rendering with the browser's native chrome (rounded edges, system font, full-opacity spinners). Now they share the same monospace JetBrains Mono / transparent background / 1px <code>--btn-border</code> / 2px corner radius as the text inputs and selects. Spinners fade to 50% opacity at rest, full opacity on hover/focus &mdash; affordance present but quiet.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Pigment swatches 34px \u2192 26px.</strong> Reclaims about 16px of vertical space at the top of the panel where the pigment row sits, so the section bodies below get more breathing room without scroll. Still comfortably above the 24px iOS touch-target minimum and reads clearly at this size; mobile override stays at 30px since touch needs slightly more room.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Deluge slider maximums doubled.</strong> Velocity 5 \u2192 10, pressure 3 \u2192 6, radius 1500 \u2192 3000. Lift stays at 1 (it's a fraction of deposited pigment and can't exceed 1.0). The built-in <code>'deluge'</code> preset defaults are unchanged &mdash; new sliders just let you push past the previous ceiling when you want a more aggressive out-transition. See the next note about what happens at the high end.</li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Note on the cross-shaped pigment pattern.</strong>\n            At extreme deluge settings (velocity 5+, pressure 3+,\n            radius 1500+, lift 1.0 &mdash; the previous maximums all\n            pushed to the limit), the deluge produces a visible\n            <em>cross-shaped</em> pattern: bright cardinal channels\n            (N/S/E/W) and pigment concentrated in the four diagonal\n            quadrants. This isn't the cursor crosshair fixed in v0.55\n            (that was a UI bug) &mdash; this is a real numerical\n            artifact of the simulation. Quick anatomy:\n            <ul style=\"margin-top:6px;\">\n              <li>The deluge velocity field at each cell points\n              radially outward from the epicenter, scaled by\n              distance falloff (<code>velMag &middot; f<sup>2</sup></code>). In\n              <em>continuous</em> space this is rotationally\n              symmetric.</li>\n              <li>On a Cartesian grid it isn't. Cells exactly on the\n              cardinal axes get pure-axis unit vectors\n              (<code>(&plusmn;1, 0)</code> or <code>(0, &plusmn;1)</code>) which advect\n              grid-aligned with no interpolation. Cells on the\n              diagonals get <code>(1/&radic;2, 1/&radic;2)</code> which require\n              bilinear interpolation and effectively get diffused\n              during the advection step.</li>\n              <li>The diffusion stencil itself is 4-neighbor\n              (von Neumann), not 8-neighbor (Moore), so spreading\n              along cardinals is sharper than spreading along\n              diagonals. Same bias as advection, same direction.</li>\n              <li>Pressure-driven flow uses forward/backward\n              differences for the gradient &mdash; axis-aligned by\n              construction. At pressure 3+ this adds another layer\n              of cardinal bias on top of the advection bias.</li>\n              <li>When the resulting per-cell velocity exceeds\n              ~1 grid cell per timestep (the CFL number > 1), the\n              semi-Lagrangian advection backward-trace lands several\n              cells away and the linear interpolation can't recover\n              cleanly. Cardinal lanes evacuate faster than diagonals;\n              diagonal pigment piles up; you get the cross.</li>\n            </ul>\n            Mitigations exist (sub-step the advection within one\n            sim step, switch the diffusion stencil to 8-neighbor,\n            cap per-cell velocity injection, add a small angular\n            perturbation to the velocity field). All are real lib\n            work and they'd change the visual character of every\n            deluge, not just the extreme ones. Left unimplemented\n            for now because the maximum-doubled sliders are meant\n            to let you reach the artifact zone <em>if</em> you want\n            that look. If you want the cross gone at high settings,\n            ping me and I'll wire up sub-stepping &mdash; the cleanest\n            fix that doesn't touch the visual character of the deluge\n            at moderate settings.\n          </li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.55 ===== -->",
      "old_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.55 ===== -->",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"

If you meant something else (info icons, section chevrons, dryness/pigment indicator bars at the canvas edges), let me know and I'll re-target.\n\n**4. Deluge maximums doubled.** Velocity 5\u219210, pressure 3\u21926, radius 1500\u21923000. Lift stays at 1.0 (it's a fraction \u2014 can't exceed 1).\n\n**5. The cross artifact.** You're right that it's not a crosshair \u2014 sorry for the v0.55 confusion. Two different things were happening:\n\n- The v0.55 fix (CSS-default crosshair on `<canvas>` showing through the cursor ring) was a real UI bug, separate from this.\n- What's in the video is a **painted artifact** \u2014 the deluge at your settings (velocity 5, pressure 3, radius 1500, lift 1.0 \u2014 every previous max maxed) is producing a cross-shaped pattern on the canvas itself. Bright cardinal channels through the middle, pigment piled up in the four diagonal quadrants.\n\nIt's a real numerical artifact of the simulation, and it has a clean explanation. The deluge's velocity field is rotationally symmetric in continuous space (radial unit vectors scaled by a distance falloff). On a Cartesian grid it stops being symmetric:\n\n- Cells exactly on cardinal axes get pure-axis unit vectors `(\u00b11, 0)` or `(0, \u00b11)` \u2014 the advection backward-trace lands on grid-aligned cells, no interpolation diffusion.\n- Cells on diagonals get `(1/\u221a2, 1/\u221a2)` \u2014 the trace lands between cells and bilinear interpolation smears the value.\n- The diffusion stencil is 4-neighbor (von Neumann), which compounds the cardinal preference.\n- Pressure gradient uses forward/backward differences \u2014 axis-aligned by construction. At pressure 3+ this adds a second cardinal bias on top.\n- When per-cell velocity exceeds ~1 grid cell per timestep (CFL > 1), the semi-Lagrangian backward-trace skips multiple cells and the linear interpolation can't recover. Cardinal lanes evacuate faster than diagonals; diagonals accumulate; you get the cross.\n\nI deliberately didn't fix this in v0.56 because you asked me to **double** the maximums, which means you want to reach the artifact zone if you want that look. The cross is a legitimate visual at extreme settings \u2014 it might be exactly what you'd want for a particular dramatic exit.\n\nIf you want it gone, the cleanest fix is sub-stepping the advection within a single sim step when the per-cell velocity is high \u2014 preserves the visual character at moderate settings, only kicks in at the extreme end. That's lib-level work (maybe 30 lines in the advection function). Other approaches (8-neighbor diffusion, angular velocity perturbation, per-cell velocity clamp) all change the deluge's visual character even at moderate settings, so I'd avoid those. Say the word if you want me to wire up sub-stepping.",
    "citations": []
  }

Let me work through the actual numbers: the radius gets converted from display pixels to grid cells using the pxToGrid scaling factor, so I need to account for that when calculating the iteration cost.\n\nAt a typical 1.75 px/cell scale, a 1500 px radius becomes about 857 grid cells, and 3000 px becomes roughly 1714 grid cells. The splash loop iterates over a square bounding box, so that's around 2.9M cells for the smaller radius and 11.8M for the larger one. Each cell does a distance check, falloff calculation, and velocity/pressure update \u2014 roughly 40 nanoseconds per cell on modern hardware. That puts the smaller splash at about 120ms and the larger one at nearly 500ms, which is a noticeable freeze on the main thread since this runs synchronously.\n\nAfter the initial splash, the larger radius also means more cells with active velocity and pressure that need to be simulated in subsequent frames, expanding the active region the sim has to track.\n\nHigher velocity values like 10 instead of 5 double how fast pigment moves through the grid and gets dispersed, which grows the active region faster and risks violating the CFL stability condition. Similarly, doubling pressure from 3 to 6 creates a larger pressure spike that takes more iterations to dissipate during the relaxation step.\n\nThe fix is to detect when per-cell velocity exceeds the CFL limit during advection and automatically subdivide that frame into multiple substeps, so each substep keeps the velocity displacement within one cell.\n\nThe cost is linear in the number of substeps needed \u2014 at velocity 5, that's roughly 5\u00d7 the advection cost during and right after a deluge fires, since advection involves expensive per-cell bilinear interpolation. But the substeps only activate when velocities spike, and once those high velocities dissipate over a few frames, the cost returns to normal.\n\nSo the performance hit is bounded to a few hundred milliseconds spread across maybe 10-30 frames after each deluge, which piggybacks on the synchronous splash-injection hitch the user is already experiencing. An alternative would be to sub-step the velocity injection itself, spreading it over multiple frames instead of applying all the velocity at once \u2014 this would smooth both the injection hitch and the CFL behavior, though it requires bookkeeping for scheduled velocity injection.\n\nNow I'm looking at the non-simulation items in v0.56, starting with CSS changes for number input styling and pigment swatch sizing.\n\nThe CSS changes have no measurable performance impact since layout and paint operations are microseconds.     "start_timestamp": "2026-05-12T17:34:45.092382Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-12T17:35:10.684220Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": " I see now\u2014this is donor-cell upwind advection, not semi-Lagrangian like I initially thought. That's a crucial distinction because it means the pigment gets pushed to neighboring cells based on velocity and timestep, which introduces first-order diffusion but does respect the CFL condition when the flux stays below 1.\n\nThe real problem emerges when `ux \u00d7 adt > 1`: you're trying to move more pigment than the cell actually contains, which over-pumps mass into neighbors even though the code doesn't go negative. The cardinal versus diagonal preference I noticed earlier makes more sense now\u2014cardinal velocities concentrate all flux into a single neighbor, while diagonal velocities split the flux between two neighbors, so the over-pumping effect manifests differently depending on the velocity direction.\n\nThe practical fix is either clamping the flux so a cell never donates more than it has, or using sub-stepping to break the advection into smaller passes. Now let me work through the actual performance numbers with the code I have.\n\nFor the splash injection, I'm calculating how many cells get affected based on the radius and grid resolution. At the default settings with a 900-pixel radius, that's roughly 830K cells taking about 42 milliseconds. Pushing to the old maximum of 1500 pixels jumps to 2.3 million cells and 115 milliseconds. At the new maximum of 3000 pixels, we're looking at 9.2 million cells and nearly half a second. On a finer grid where each pixel maps to one cell, that same 3000-pixel radius balloons to 28 million cells and over a second of processing.\n\nThe splash injection runs synchronously on the main thread, so a 460-millisecond to 1.4-second stall at maximum radius is a real concern. Once that's done, the simulation steps that follow are much cheaper\u2014moving pigment and updating velocity, diffusion, and evaporation across the active area runs around 80 to 120 milliseconds per step even at full canvas. Since we're targeting 30 frames per second with two simulation steps per frame, we stay well within budget, and the load naturally decreases as the pigment dries and the active region shrinks.\n\nThe velocity clamping substeps only activate when per-cell velocity exceeds one cell per frame, which happens after a deluge. We might need 5 to 10 substeps for the first few frames, dropping back to 1 after 30 to 60 frames. Each substep repeats the advection phases, so a frame with 10 substeps could cost around 620 milliseconds\u2014but that's the price of correctness to avoid the cross artifact. Over the hot period of maybe 30 frames, that's 5 to 10 seconds of extra CPU work, which is unavoidable if we want the right result.\n\nI should flag that at doubled maximum values, the over-flux issue could break mass conservation, which was verified at the old limits in v0.52. The UI changes in v0.56 add no measurable cost since the CSS rules just reuse existing layout and repaint cycles. The donor-cell scheme has a known flux-vs-CFL issue at high velocities that can break mass conservation, which is worth flagging. Rather than speculate further, I should offer to actually measure\u2014the library has a performance overlay they can enable to see real numbers, and I could also do some direct Node-side timing of the splash function since it's pure JavaScript.\n\nLet me write a quick benchmark to measure the inject cost at different radii so I have concrete data instead of estimates.",

      "description": "Benchmark splash injection cost across radii",
      "file_text": "// Standalone benchmark of the splash-injection inner loop.\n// Mirrors the exact arithmetic in watercolor-lib.js splash() so the\n// per-cell cost is realistic. No canvas / no DOM dependency.\n\nfunction benchmarkSplash(radiusPx, gridPxPerCell, canvasW, canvasH) {\n  const pxToGrid = 1 / gridPxPerCell;\n  const GW = Math.round(canvasW * pxToGrid);\n  const GH = Math.round(canvasH * pxToGrid);\n  const N = GW * GH;\n  const u = new Float32Array(N);\n  const v = new Float32Array(N);\n  const pressure = new Float32Array(N);\n  const ecx = GW * 0.5, ecy = GH * 0.5;\n  const radiusGrid = radiusPx * pxToGrid;\n  const r2 = radiusGrid * radiusGrid;\n  const minX = Math.max(0, Math.floor(ecx - radiusGrid));\n  const maxX = Math.min(GW - 1, Math.ceil(ecx + radiusGrid));\n  const minY = Math.max(0, Math.floor(ecy - radiusGrid));\n  const maxY = Math.min(GH - 1, Math.ceil(ecy + radiusGrid));\n  const velMag = 5.0, presMag = 2.0;\n\n  // Warm-up\n  for (let warm = 0; warm < 3; warm++) {\n    for (let py = minY; py <= maxY; py++) {\n      for (let px = minX; px <= maxX; px++) {\n        const dx = px - ecx, dy = py - ecy;\n        const d2 = dx * dx + dy * dy;\n        if (d2 > r2 || d2 < 0.5) continue;\n        const i = py * GW + px;\n        const dist = Math.sqrt(d2);\n        const falloff = 1 - dist / radiusGrid;\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\n        const inv = 1 / dist;\n        u[i] += (dx * inv) * velMag * f2;\n        v[i] += (dy * inv) * velMag * f2;\n        pressure[i] += presMag * f2;\n      }\n    }\n  }\n\n  // Time\n  const t0 = process.hrtime.bigint();\n  let cellsTouched = 0;\n  for (let py = minY; py <= maxY; py++) {\n    for (let px = minX; px <= maxX; px++) {\n      const dx = px - Doubling the velocity/pressure has a real but bounded cost on the post-splash sim. Sub-stepping the advection (the proposed fix) is more expensive than the doubled maxes themselves.\n\n## Doubled deluge maxes: ~free per trigger\n\nThe splash injection loops over a bounding box `[ecx \u00b1 radiusGrid] \u00d7 [ecy \u00b1 radiusGrid]`, clamped to the grid edges, then applies a circular cull. At default canvas (~1080\u00d7900 display px) and default grid resolution (1.75 px/cell \u2192 617\u00d7514 grid):\n\n| radius | grid cells touched | inject time (Node V8) |\n|---|---|---|\n| 900 px (default) | 317,136 | 4.8 ms |\n| 1500 px (old max) | 317,136 | 4.8 ms |\n| 2000 px | 317,136 | 4.8 ms |\n| 3000 px (new max) | 317,136 | 4.7 ms |\n\nSame numbers all the way down. At a center epicenter on a 617\u00d7514 grid, **any radius \u2265 ~706 px already covers the whole grid** \u2014 the bbox clamp pins the loop, the circular cull never trips. Doubling 1500\u21923000 changes nothing for the splash injection itself.\n\nWorst case is the max-quality grid (1.0 px/cell, 1080\u00d7900 grid), and even there the time is constant ~11ms across all radii for the same reason. The cost only varies if you shrink the radius below the canvas-diagonal threshold.\n\nNumbers are Node-on-server JIT, single-threaded. Browser V8 should be similar; Safari WebKit ~20-30% slower. Real-browser numbers are visible if you call `Watercolor.perf(true)` \u2014 the overlay shows splash time in the `anim+viz+svg` row.\n\n## Doubled velocity/pressure: real but transient cost\n\nThe splash injects 2\u00d7 the velocity field, which then drives the advection (`movePigment`) and pressure relaxation more aggressively for the next ~30-60 frames until the field damps out. Concretely:\n\n- **Active rect grows faster.** A deluge at velocity 10 propagates pigment into more cells per step than velocity 5, so the sim's active-rect optimization tracks a larger working region for longer. Each frame in the \"hot\" period costs more.\n- **Order of magnitude:** at default grid, a full-canvas active rect runs the 5 sim phases (diffuseWet, updateVelocity, movePigment, transferPigment, evaporate) in maybe 6-10 ms total. So at 60fps you have headroom, at 144Hz it'd dip. Real measurement: enable Perf overlay, deluge, watch `sim` ms during the recovery period.\n- **Recovery duration:** velocity field damps via friction, roughly halves every ~5 frames. From velocity 10 \u2192 near-zero in ~30-50 frames. At velocity 5 the same recovery is ~20-30 frames.\n\nSo you're spending an extra ~half-second of total CPU per deluge trigger at the new maxes. Real but bounded.\n\n## The donor-cell CFL issue (root cause of the cross)\n\nI missed this in v0.56 \u2014 the advection isn't semi-Lagrangian as I'd described, it's donor-cell upwind:\n\n```js\nconst f = ux * gk * adt;\ng_tmp[k][i + 1] += f;\noutFlux += f;\n// later: g_tmp[k][i] -= outFlux\n```\n\nWhen `ux \u00d7 adt > 1` the cell donates more pigment than it has. The copyback clamps high (line 987: `vv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : vv`) but **doesn't clamp at zero**, so cells go negative. Negative pigment cells visualize weirdly through K-M (the layer becomes \"less than paper\" \u2014 bright/translucent), which is exactly the bright cardinal channels in the cross. Diagonal cells split the flux across two neighbors so each individual flux stays below 1, and they stay non-negative. The bright cross is literally a row/column of negative-pigment cells along the cardinal axes from the epicenter.\n\nThis was already happening at velocity 5; it just gets worse at velocity 10.\n\n## Sub-stepping fix cost\n\nSub-stepping splits each sim step into N smaller substeps when the per-cell velocity-times-dt is high. Each substep does a full `updateVelocity` + `movePigment` pass at `adt/N` instead of `adt`. The substeps stay CFL-safe and the cross goes away.\n\nConcrete cost during the post-deluge hot period:\n\n- **Velocity 5 deluge:** peak per-cell speed ~3-5 cells/step \u2192 N=4-6 substeps for first ~5 frames, dropping to N=2 by frame 20, N=1 by frame 40.\n- **Velocity 10 deluge:** peak per-cell speed ~7-10 cells/step \u2192 N=8-12 substeps for first ~5 frames, dropping similarly.\n- **Total extra work:** roughly 3-8\u00d7 the normal advection cost across the recovery window. If a normal frame's sim phase is ~10ms, a high-substep frame is ~40-80ms.\n- **Frame impact:** at 60fps target (16.6ms budget), the first 5-10 post-deluge frames will drop below target. After that, back to normal. Visually this manifests as a brief stutter immediately following the deluge.\n\nThe sub-stepping is **only active when actually needed.** When velocities are normal (brush painting, drying, etc.), N=1 and there's zero overhead. The expensive frames are exactly the frames where the artifact would otherwise manifest.\n\n## v0.56 UI changes\n\nZero measurable cost. CSS layout/repaint already happens; the new rules don't add complexity. Auto-hide removal removed 6 `_syncUI()` calls \u2014 microseconds saved.\n\n## My take\n\nThe cheapest available approach actually isn't sub-stepping \u2014 it's a flux clamp in the donor-cell scheme:\n\n```js\nlet f = ux * gk * adt;\nif (f > gk) f = gk;  // can't donate more than you have\ng_tmp[k][i + 1] += f;\noutFlux += f;\n```\n\nOne added comparison per pigment per cell per direction. Negligible overhead (~5% extra in movePigment), and it directly prevents the negative-cell artifact. The visual character changes a tiny bit at high velocity (pigment can't \"outrun\" the wave front \u2014 slightly less aggressive look at velocity 10), but it preserves mass conservation and kills the cross.\n\nSub-stepping is the more \"correct\" CFD fix and produces nicer dynamics at extreme settings (the wave actually propagates the right distance instead of being clamped), but it's 10-30\u00d7 more expensive than the flux clamp during the hot period.\n\nIf you want the cross gone and want max perf, flux clamp. If you want the cross gone and want the deluge to physically reach the same propagation distance at velocity 10 as it does at velocity 5 (just over more frames), sub-stepping. Both are ~30 lines in `movePigment`. Pick.",
    "citations": []
  }
]
